---
title: "Metal & Enchanting-Based Money Supply"
output: html_notebook
---

```{r Gold, Silver, and Copper Money Supply one-off}
##**Coin Production***##
#Assuming all gold is produced in Country X, all gold is of the same grade, and miners are paid a set wage.

#Miners produce 1 unit of gold per week
#There are L miners total, with L_i miners mining ore i where i is in the set {g,s,c}
L <- 100
L_g <- floor(1/3 * L)
L_s <- floor(1/3 * L)
L_c <- floor(1/3 * L)
#Gold Produced in a week
g <- 1 * L_g
s <- 1 * L_s
c <- 1 * L_c
#Miners paid 1 enchanted gold coin per week
wage_g <- 1
wage_s <- 1
wage_c <- 1
#cost of Mining Gold
glaborcost <- wage_g * L_g
slaborcost <- wage_s * L_s
claborcost <- wage_c * L_c
##**Minting Gold**##
#Assume e is the percent of a unit of gold lost in minting

e <- runif(1, 0, 0.0002)
#For every unit of gold, silver, or copper, x coins can be minted (with spillage accounted for)
x_g <- 10
x_s <- 10
x_c <- 10
M_g <- floor(x_g * g * (1-e))
M_s <- floor(x_s * s * (1-e))
M_c <- floor(x_c * c * (1-e))

#Cost of minting
gmintcost <- 10
smintcost <- 10
cmintcost <- 10

#Spillage and loss accounting
## When implementing repeat simulations, keep stock of difference in M_g with and without floor() to accumulate so less loss

spill_g <- x_g * g - M_g
spill_s <- x_s * s - M_s
spill_c <- x_c * c - M_c

loss_g <- spill_g * 1
loss_s <- spill_s * 0.1
loss_c <- spill_c * 0.01


##**Enchanting Gold**##
#*Country Y is sent M coins to enchant, which they do with a success rate of a
a_g <- 1
a_c <- 1
a_s <- 1

g_enchant <- a_g * M_g
s_enchant <- a_s * M_s
c_enchant <- a_c * M_c
##**Returning Gold*##
#Country Y keeps y% of the coins enchanted as payment since they are the only ones capable of this service.
y_g <- 0.1
y_s <- 0.1
y_c <- 0.1

greserve <- y_g * g_enchant
sreserve <- y_s * s_enchant
creserve <- y_c * c_enchant

gsupply <- g_enchant - greserve
ssupply <- s_enchant - sreserve
csupply <- c_enchant - creserve

#Repaying the cost to create this, we have the following cost 
gcost <- glaborcost + gmintcost
scost <- slaborcost + smintcost
ccost <- claborcost + smintcost
#Finally, the actual value of the generated Money Supply in terms of gold
moneysupply <- (gsupply * 1 - gcost) +  (ssupply * 0.1 - scost) + (csupply * 0.001 - ccost)
moneysupply
```
```{r Repeated Simulation of Gold Production}
##**Mining Gold***##
#Assuming all gold is produced in Country X, all gold is of the same grade, and miners are paid a set wage.

#Miners produce 1 unit of gold per week
#There are L miners total, with L_g miners mining gold
L <- 100
L_c <- floor(1/3 * L)

#Gold Produced in a week
G <- 1*L
#Miners paid 1 enchanted gold coin per week
w <- 1
#cost of Mining Gold
c_l <- w*L_g
##**Minting Gold**##
#Assume e is the percent of a unit of gold lost in minting

e <- runif(1, 0, 0.0002)
#For every unit of gold, x coins can be minted (with spillage accounted for)
x <- 10
M <- floor(x*G*(1-e))
#Cost of minting
c_m <- 10
##**Enchanting Gold**##
#*Country Y is sent M coins to enchant, which they do with 100% success rate
a <- 1
E <- a*M
##**Returning Gold*##
#Country Y keeps y% of the coins as payment, and as they are the only ones capable of this service.
y <- 0.1
R <- floor(E*y)
Q <- E - R

#Repaying the cost to create this, we have the following cost 
C <- c_l + c_m
#Finally, the actual Money Supply is
S <- Q - C
S
```
